Green hydrogen for a sustainable future: A review of production methods, innovations, and applications
======================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel C.Anand a, B.Chandraja a, P.Nithiya a, M.Akshaya a, P.Tamizhdurai a, G.Shoba b, A.Subramani c, R.Kumaran a, Krishna Kumar Yadav d e, Amel Gacem f, Javed Khan Bhutto g, Maha Awjan Alreshidi h, Mir Waqas Alam i

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2025.02.257](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2025.02.257 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S0360319925008341&orderBeanReset=true)

Highlights
----------

*   •This article traces about the pathway of Green Hydrogen & history of Hydrogen. 
*   •How Hydrogen is going to decarbonize the fossil fuel based world in sustainable way. 
*   •A brief explanation about types of Hydrogen based on production method. 
*   •Exclusively about Carbon neutral Green Hydrogen and its production method-water electrolysis. 
*   •How seawater and wastewater acts as a potential source for Green Hydrogen production. 

Abstract
--------

Green hydrogen is essential for advancing sustainable energy systems and achieving a low-carbon economy. Various production methods have been developed, each with distinct technological characteristics, efficiency levels, and environmental impacts. Conventional and advanced approaches include water electrolysis such as alkaline electrolysis, proton exchange membrane (PEM) electrolysis, and solid oxide electrolysis (SOE)—as well as innovative processes like biological hydrogen production through biophotolysis, fermentation, biological water-gas shift reactions, and microbial fuel cells. Other significant techniques encompass thermochemical hydrogen production methods like biomass gasification, steam methane reforming, and thermochemical cycles, alongside photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting systems, including single-junction, dual-junction, integrated PEC cells, and photocatalytic PEC cells. Emerging plasma-assisted water splitting methods, such as corona discharge, dielectric barrier discharge (DBD), and microwave plasma-assisted splitting, also offer promising alternatives for efficient hydrogen production. Among these methods, water electrolysis powered by renewable electricity holds substantial potential for large-scale green hydrogen production. Photoelectrochemical water splitting and plasma-assisted methods have demonstrated unique advantages in terms of sustainability and integration with renewable energy sources. The scalability, cost-effectiveness, and environmental benefits of these processes depend on technological advancements in reactor design, materials, and catalytic efficiency. Integrating green hydrogen with carbon sequestration technologies further enhances its role in reducing carbon footprints and promoting sustainability. Sustainable applications extend to energy storage solutions, where green hydrogen can store surplus renewable energy, and transportation, particularly in fuel cell vehicles, promoting zero-emission mobility. This review examines these hydrogen production methods, highlighting their efficiencies, challenges, and recent innovations that are pivotal for achieving competitive, large-scale green hydrogen production. Furthermore, the integration of green hydrogen with carbon sequestration technologies is discussed, underscoring its role in reducing carbon footprints and enhancing sustainability.

Introduction
------------

Hydrogen, the lightest and most plentiful element in the universe, has emerged as a cornerstone in the global transition to sustainable power solutions. As a zero-emission fuel that produces only water when combusted, hydrogen has captivated worldwide attention for its potential to power a sustainable and greener future. With the depletion of crude oil reserves and the pressing need to mitigate climate change, scientific and industrial efforts are increasingly focused on renewable energy solutions. These efforts align with international agreements such as the Montreal Protocol (1987), Kyoto Protocol (1997), and Paris Agreement (2015), which emphasize the reduction of emissions of greenhouse gas and the adoption of sustainable practices [[1], [2], [3], [4]].

Hydrogen stands out for its unparalleled energy storage capacity—three times that of gasoline and seven times that of coal—making it an attractive alternative for addressing the energy demands of a growing global population. Despite fossil fuels dominating the energy market with a 70% share in 2023 and contributing to an annual release of 36.8 billion metric tons of CO 2, renewable energy transition is gaining momentum globally [5]. Solar, wind, hydro, geothermal, and biomass energy offer cleaner, more sustainable alternatives, though they face challenges like intermittency, unpredictability, and high storage costs [6]. Amid these limitations, green hydrogen, formed through water electrolysis powered by renewable electricity, has manifested as a transformative solution. It not only stores renewable energy for consistent availability but also plays a pivotal role in attaining Sustainable Development Goal 7—ensuring access to affordable, reliable, and sustainable energy [7].

While the promise of hydrogen as a clean, sustainable fuel is significant, several challenges must be addressed. Its low density, high flammability, and the complexities associated with storage and transportation pose hurdles to its widespread adoption [8,9]. Nevertheless, hydrogen's potential to decarbonize transportation, heavy industry, and power generation aligns seamlessly with global sustainability goals, making it a key player to combat climate change [10].

As hydrogen's role in the global energy mix expands, innovations in its production, storage, and distribution are critical. The development of cost-effective technologies, such as electrolysis powered by renewable energy, and the establishment of supportive infrastructure and regulatory frameworks, will be key to harnessing the full potential of the hydrogen economy by 2050. This article delves into the innovations driving green hydrogen production and examines its transformative role in shaping a sustainable future [11].

Hydrogen production methods are broadly categorized into two main approaches: reforming fossil fuels like methane and methanol, and splitting water through electrolysis [12]. Fossil fuel reforming, including steam methane reforming (SMR) and coal gasification, dominates global hydrogen production, accounting for approximately 76% of output. However, these methods are unsustainable and carbon-intensive, emitting 8–30 kg of CO 2 per kg of hydrogen produced [13]. While SMR is an endothermic and efficient process, it relies on finite natural gas reserves and has a significant impact on greenhouse gas emissions. To address these challenges, the focus must shift toward carbon-free sources such as water electrolysis, biomass gasification, and metal-water splitting.

Water, as the most promising hydrogen-rich source, offers a 2:1 hydrogen-to-oxygen ratio, and electrolysis generates no direct emissions. Biomass gasification utilizes organic material with minimal carbon footprint, while metal-water splitting employs reactive metals to facilitate hydrogen production, though its dependence on mining raises sustainability concerns [14]. Transitioning to these carbon-free methods, particularly electrolytic production of green hydrogen using renewable energy, offers a sustainable path to carbon-neutral hydrogen production, aligning with global climate goals [15,16].

Green hydrogen derived from water electrolysis using renewable power, is a highly promising energy carrier due to its unique properties. Physically, the molecular weight of hydrogen is 2.016 g/mol, making it the lightest element, and exists as a colorless, odorless, and tasteless gas. It has an extremely low density of 0.08988 kg/m 3 at standard temperature and pressure (STP), making it about 14 times lighter than air [17]. Chemically, hydrogen is highly reactive, possessing the highest energy content per unit mass among fuels at 120 MJ/kg. Its combustion produces only water, ensuring zero direct carbon emissions, which positions it as a clean alternative to fossil fuels [18]. Environmentally, green hydrogen is sustainable and produced using renewable energy, substantially cutting reliance on non-renewable resources and greenhouse gas emissions [19]. Green hydrogen production made more efficient by combining hybrid systems such as integrating renewable energy sources like solar, wind and hydro with electrolysis [7].

However, its volumetric energy density is low, necessitating advanced storage solutions such as high-pressure tanks, liquefaction, or metal hydrides. Furthermore, hydrogen's versatility allows its use in diverse applications, including energy storage, transportation, and industrial processes, as well as its function as a precursor for synthetic fuels [16]. These properties highlight the potential of green hydrogen as a cornerstone of the global transition to a sustainable and decarbonized energy future.

-----------------------

Hydrogen is classified into nine types (Fig. 1) based on production methods, sources, and carbon emissions (Table 1). Green hydrogen, produced through carbon-neutral processes like water electrolysis, aims for zero emissions, promoting decarbonization. Other types include blue hydrogen, which uses carbon-based sources like natural gas and captures 90% of emitted CO 2, making it cost-effective compared to green hydrogen [11].

Pathways to green hydrogen production
-------------------------------------

Renewable energy-driven green hydrogen production is pivotal in achieving a sustainable, carbon-neutral energy system. The most common method for producing green hydrogen is via water electrolysis, a technique where electricity is used to electrolyze water into hydrogen and oxygen. There are three main types of electrolysis used for hydrogen production. Water splitting: current technologies and sustainability benefits
-----------------------------------------------------------------

Water splitting is a crucial methodology for creating green hydrogen, providing a carbon-free alternative to conventional hydrogen production methods. In this process, electrolysis splits water (H 2 O) into hydrogen (H 2) and oxygen (O 2). When powered by renewable energy resources such as solar, wind, or hydroelectric power, the electrolysis process generates no carbon emissions, establishing it as an environmentally viable solution for clean energy production. Biological hydrogen production
------------------------------

Biological hydrogen production is a sustainable method that harnesses the capabilities of microorganisms, such as bacteria, algae, and cyanobacteria, to produce green hydrogen. This process occurs through various mechanisms, including fermentation, photosynthesis, and microbial electrolysis. Photoelectrochemical water splitting
------------------------------------

PEC water splitting is an emerging technology for green hydrogen production by employing solar energy to fragment water molecules into hydrogen and oxygen. The process occurs in a photoelectrochemical cell (PEC cell), where semiconductor materials (photoelectrodes) absorb light and drive electrochemical reactions [124]. -----------------------------------------------------------

Green hydrogen, created through the electrolysis of water using renewable energy sources, is evolving into a key solution in the drive toward a sustainable future. Its applications span a wide range of sectors was depicted in Fig. 11, each offering significant environmental and economic benefits. Role of green hydrogen in promoting sustainability development goals and carbon sequestration
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Hydrogen, particularly green hydrogen, is increasingly validated as a significant player in the transition towards a sustainable, low-carbon energy future. Its potential to transform various sectors especially those heavily reliant on fossil fuels supports multiple Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), particularly in mitigate climate change, promoting clean energy, and foster economic growth.

Conclusions
-----------

Green hydrogen production represents a promising pathway for achieving a decarbonized energy landscape. Among the various techniques, water electrolysis, particularly when coupled with renewable energy sources, remains a cornerstone for sustainable hydrogen generation. Alkaline, PEM, and SOE are well-established methods with potential for scaling, but they still face challenges related to cost and efficiency. enriched_points.expanded_content: Conducting a comprehensive literature review allows for the synthesis of existing knowledge on green hydrogen production methods, identifying advancements and gaps in current research and technologies.

Employing diverse data collection methods, including surveys and experimental data, enables the gathering of quantitative and qualitative insights essential for analyzing the efficiency and effectiveness of production reactors.

Analyzing relevant case studies provides practical examples of successful green hydrogen production systems, illustrating real-world applications, challenges faced, and solutions implemented in various operational contexts.
